topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was a physical weight, pressing down on my chest until I could barely draw a breath. I stared at the empty chair across the mahogany desk, the expensive leather still cool beneath my fingertips. He was gone. Just… gone. No note, no farewell, just an abrupt vacuum where his presence used to be. My jaw tightened until a dull ache radiated through my molars. I stood up, the scrape of my chair protesting loudly in the quiet room. Every instinct screamed for me to demand an explanation, to force some justification for this sudden vanishing act. I clenched my fists, the knuckles turning white, and walked toward the door, the sound of my own hurried footsteps echoing the frantic rhythm of my rising frustration.

[story 2]

I reread the final email, the one confirming his retirement date, the cheerful sign-off utterly devoid of warmth. A slow, hot burn started low in my stomach and spread outward, making my hands tremble slightly as I gripped the edge of my desk. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a profound sense of being dismissed, of having poured years of dedication into a void. I slammed the file shut, the sound sharp and jarring in the quiet workspace. I paced the length of the room twice, my movements jerky and sharp. When I finally stopped, I leaned my forehead against the cool surface of the filing cabinet, breathing in ragged, shallow gasps, the tension coiling tight in my shoulders.

[story 3]

"You're kidding me," I muttered, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I couldn't shake the image of him, mid-sentence, looking eager and brilliant, now just… absent. A deep, churning restlessness took hold of me, making my skin prickle with a restless energy. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city below, feeling a powerful surge of indignation. My voice, when I finally spoke, was dangerously low, strained with barely contained force. "Where is he? Why didn't he even tell me?" The sheer unfairness of it all made my breath hitch, a tight, involuntary catch in my throat, as I turned away from the view, unable to look at anything else.
